New Chinese AI agent destroys Notebook Lem. Forget everything you know about online learning. A brand new AI just dropped from one of China's top universities. It builds you a full interactive classroom from any topic or PDF in one click with AI teachers, debates, quizzes, and whiteboards. And it just went open source. This changes everything. Okay, so let's talk about what just happened because this is one of those moments where you see something and you think, "Wait, why didn't this exist already?" Tingua University, which is basically the MIT of China, just open- sourced a project called OpenMEch. And on the surface, it sounds simple. You give it a topic or a PDF and it builds you a classroom. But when you actually dig into what that classroom looks like, it's nothing like what you'd expect. We're not talking about a chatbot that summarizes your notes. We're not talking about some flashcard generator. This is a full multi- aent AI system that simulates an actual class. You've got an AI teacher leading the session. You've got peer agents that debate each other. You've got quizzes that check your understanding in real time. You've got a whiteboard. It feels like being in a real classroom, except the whole thing was built for you in one click from any topic you choose. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below. And here's the first open loop I want to leave you with. Later in this video, I'm going to show you exactly how something like this could be used to train your team, onboard new staff, or teach your clients how to use AI automation tools. So stick around because that part is genuinely useful for anyone running a business right now. But first, let me give you the full picture on what openmech actually is and where it came from. The project comes out of Singa University's MAC initiative, which stands for multi-agent AI for collaborative learning. The core idea is that learning is not a passive experience. When you're in a real classroom, you're not just listening to one person talk at you. You're watching other students ask questions. You're seeing debates happen. You're getting challenged on what you think you understand. That dynamic is what makes learning stick. The problem with most AI learning tools, including Notebook LM, which a lot of people love, is that they're still pretty one-directional. You feed it content, it summarizes or explains it back to you, which is useful, but it's not the same as actually being in a class. Open May tried to solve that, and the way they built it is genuinely clever. The system used its large language models, including Google's Gemini, to power different agents that each play a role inside the classroom. The teacher agent drives the session. The peer agents act like other students who are learning alongside you, asking questions, pushing back on ideas, making the session feel alive. The whole thing is orchestrated so that it mimics the rhythm of a real class rather than just a Q&amp;A session with a chatbot. And this is exactly the kind of tool we break down inside the AI profit boardroom. The best place to scale your business, get more customers, and save hundreds of hours with AI automation. We go deep on tools like openmake. So you're not just reading about them, you're actually building with them. If you want in the link is in the description and the comments and they didn't just build it and theorize about whether it works, they actually tested it. In a study with 700 students, 84% reported enjoying the open make sessions. And the assessment scores came out better than traditional classes. That's not a small sample. That's real data showing that this approach actually moves the needle on learning outcomes. Now it's open source under the Ajax 3.0 license and it's already exploding on GitHub. Over 1,400 stars in a short window. Developers are building machine learning lessons with it, exploring complex topics, praising the fact that it actually feels like a classroom rather than a glorified search engine. Okay, so now let me show you why this matters for you. Specifically, if you're running a business and using AI automation, think about the AI profit boardroom. One of the things we do inside there is help business owners get up to speed on AI tools fast. Not theory, practical stuff. How to actually use these tools to save time, get more customers and automate the boring parts of running a business. Now, imagine you could take any concept we teach inside the boardroom. Let's say how to use AI agents for content creation, and you drop it into OpenME. In one click, you get a full interactive classroom built around that topic. your team members, your new hires, your clients, they go through a structured session with an AI teacher. They get quizzed. They see debates between peer agents about the best approach and they come out the other side actually understanding the material rather than just having skimmed a PDF. That's not a hypothetical future use case. That's something you could literally deploy today because this is open source and free to use. Now, let me get into the technical side because the architecture here is actually worth understanding even if you're not a developer. Open Mayarch is built as a multi- aent system. That means instead of one AI doing everything, you have specialized agents that each have a job. There's the teacher agent, there's the peer agents, there's the assessment layer, and they all talk to each other inside the session in a way that feels coordinated rather than chaotic. What makes it flexible is the multiLM support. You're not locked into one model. Right now, it works with Gemini and the team has built it so that other models can be plugged in. This matters because it means as new and better models come out, the classroom quality improves without you having to rebuild anything. You just swap the model. The deployment is also straightforward enough that developers are already praising how quick it is to get running. You don't need a team of engineers. You can spin it up, point it at a topic or a document, and it handles the rest. Here's the second open loop. I want to talk about what this means for the future of AI education and why open make might just be the beginning of something much bigger. because there's a direction this is all heading that most people haven't fully connected yet and it's going to change how businesses train people at scale. But before we get there, let me walk you through a real example of how I'd actually use this. Let's say I want to train a new member of the Goldie agency team on the basics of link building and SEO. Normally that means I record a video or I write a dock or I have someone sit with them for a day. That works, but it doesn't scale. And the new person is mostly passive the whole time. They watch, they read, they nod. With OpenMake, I'd take our internal link, builds in SOPs, drop them into the system, and it would build a classroom around them. The new team member goes through a session where the AI teacher explains the concepts. Peer agents debate the right approach to different linku built-in scenarios and quizzes check whether they actually understand the strategy or just think they do. That is a fundamentally better onboarding experience, and it takes me almost no time to set up. Same thing for client education. If you work with clients who need to understand how AI automation can help their business, you don't have to spend hours on calls explaining the same concepts over and over. You build them a classroom, they go through it, they come out informed. Your calls become strategy conversations rather than basic explainers. This is how AI automation actually saves you time in the real world. Not by doing a task for you once, but by building systems that remove the repetitive parts of your work permanently. Now, let me come back to that open loop about where this is all heading. The reason open make matters beyond just being a cool tool is what it signals about the direction of AI agents in education and training. For the last couple of years, we've been in a phase where AI tools mostly help individuals do tasks faster. Write this summarize that generate this image. What open maker represents is the next phase multi- aent systems that simulate environments. Not just giving you an output but building you an experience. And once you can simulate a classroom, you can simulate other complex environments too. A sales training scenario, a customer service roleplay, a strategic planning session.
